sections.heading: Surgery
sections.content:
Surgery is a major component of treatment for locoregional esophageal and EGJ cancers. Esophagectomy is the primary treatment for patients with T1b or greater tumors. Preoperative chemoradiation or perioperative chemotherapy is recommended for locally advanced resectable disease to improve survival, reduce locoregional recurrence, and increase R0 resection rates. Surgery is also considered for palliation of symptoms such as dysphagia or fistula.

Principles:
1. All patients should be evaluated for physiologic ability to undergo major abdominal and/or thoracic surgery.
2. Clinical staging with CT, FDG-PET/CT, and EUS should be performed prior to surgery to assess resectability.
3. Siewert tumor type should be assessed in all patients with adenocarcinomas involving the EGJ. Siewert Type I and II tumors are managed as esophageal cancers; Type III as gastric cancers.
4. Esophageal resection should be performed in high-volume centers by experienced surgeons.
5. Minimally invasive esophagectomy (MIE) approaches should be strongly considered given improved postoperative outcomes compared to open surgery.
6. Gastric conduit is preferred for reconstruction. Colon or jejunum may be used if the stomach is unavailable.
7. At least 16 regional lymph nodes should be removed and pathologically examined during resection for curative intent.
8. Feeding jejunostomy for postoperative nutritional support is generally preferred over gastrostomy, which may compromise gastric conduit vasculature.

Resectability criteria: Resectable esophageal or EGJ cancer includes T1-T3 tumors, even with regional nodal metastases (N+), although bulky, multistation nodal involvement is a relative contraindication. T4a tumors (involving pleura, pericardium, diaphragm, or azygos vein) are resectable. Unresectable disease includes cT4b tumors (involving heart, great vessels, trachea, vertebral body, or adjacent organs like liver, pancreas, lung, spleen), most patients with multistation, bulky lymphadenopathy, EGJ tumors with supraclavicular lymph node involvement, and distant metastases (M1). Positive peritoneal cytology (without visible implants) is considered M1 disease.

Pre-operative optimisation: Patients should be assessed by an esophageal surgeon for physiologic ability to tolerate major surgery. Nutritional assessment and counseling are essential; enteric feeding tube (jejunostomy preferred) should be considered for patients with significant dysphagia/weight loss to support nutrition during induction therapy. Smoking cessation is strongly encouraged. Perioperative cardiopul

cards.name: Ivor Lewis esophagogastrectomy
cards.detail: Approach: Laparotomy + right thoracotomy. Indications: Distal thoracic esophageal or EGJ tumors. Key complications: Anastomotic leak, stricture, pneumonia, recurrent laryngeal nerve injury. Operative mortality: Reported mortality rates range from 1-5% in high-volume centers.

cards.name: McKeown esophagogastrectomy
cards.detail: Approach: Right thoracotomy + laparotomy + cervical anastomosis. Indications: Upper, middle, or lower thoracic esophageal tumors. Allows for more extensive esophageal resection. Key complications: Anastomotic leak (higher rate than intrathoracic), stricture, recurrent laryngeal nerve injury, wound infection. Operative mortality: Reported mortality rates range from 1-5% in high-volume centers.

cards.name: Minimally invasive esophagectomy (MIE)
cards.detail: Approach: Includes minimally invasive Ivor Lewis (laparoscopy + limited right thoracotomy) and minimally invasive McKeown (right thoracoscopy + limited laparotomy/laparoscopy + cervical anastomosis). Robotic-assisted MIE is an emerging technique. Indications: Early-stage disease, or as a standard approach for resectable tumors in suitable patients. May be preferred for older patients or those with comorbidities. Key complications: Similar to open approaches but with potentially lower rates of pulmonary complications, shorter hospital stay, and faster recovery. Operative mortality: Reported mortality rates as low as 1-2% in high-volume centers.

cards.name: Transhiatal esophagogastrectomy
cards.detail: Approach: Laparotomy + cervical anastomosis. Indications: Selected distal esophageal or EGJ tumors. Not recommended as a routine approach due to lower lymph node retrieval. Key complications: Anastomotic leak, stricture, recurrent laryngeal nerve injury, incomplete mediastinal dissection. Operative mortality: May have lower early morbidity but long-term survival similar to transthoracic approaches.

cards.name: Left transthoracic or thoracoabdominal esophagogastrectomy
cards.detail: Approach: Contiguous abdominal and left thoracic incision through the eighth intercostal space. Indications: Selected distal esophageal or EGJ tumors, particularly bulky tumors. Key complications: Anastomotic leak, stricture, pneumonia. Operative mortality: Similar to other transthoracic approaches.
